items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[🔧 STILL IN PROGRESS\]

*Name:* Halton Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Cheshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 11th-13th century
*Coords:* 53.3331, -2.6957
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Halton_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Halton%20Castle/@53.3333,-2.6833,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Halton_Castle_%285%29.JPG/330px-Halton_Castle_%285%29.JPG
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* unknown
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* no google places. 

*Description:*
Halton Castle is a castle in the village of Halton, part of the town of Runcorn, Cheshire, England. The castle is on the top of Halton Hill, a sandstone prominence overlooking the village. The original building, a motte-and-bailey castle began in 1071, was replaced with the current sandstone castle in the 13th century. Building alterations continued until at least 1609, when the structure is recorded as in disrepair. The castle is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building, and a scheduled monument.

_Listing 1 of 10_

*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/South_facade_of_Lyme_Park_house%2C_2013.jpg/330px-South_facade_of_Lyme_Park_house%2C_2013.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* unknown
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Lyme Park is a large estate south of Disley, Cheshire, England. It is managed by the National Trust and consists of a mansion house surrounded by formal gardens and a deer park in the Peak District National Park. The house is the largest in Cheshire and is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building.

_Listing 2 of 10_

*County:* Cheshire
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 12th-16th century
*Coords:* 53.3423, -2.6799
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Norton_Priory
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Norton%20Priory/@53.35,-2.5667,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Norton_Priory.jpg/330px-Norton_Priory.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* add source - https://www.nortonpriory.org/

*Description:*
Norton Priory is a historic site in Norton, Runcorn, Cheshire, England, comprising the remains of an abbey complex dating from the 12th to 16th centuries, and an 18th-century country house; it is now a museum. The remains are a scheduled ancient monument and are recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building. They are considered to be the most important monastic remains in Cheshire.

_Listing 3 of 10_

listing.name: Norton Priory
listing.desc: Norton Priory is a historic site in Norton, Runcorn, Cheshire, England, comprising the remains of an abbey complex dating from the 12th to 16th centuries, and an 18th-century country house; it is now a museum. The remains are a scheduled ancient monument and are recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building. They are considered to be the most important monastic remains in Cheshire.

*Name:* Whalley Abbey
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Lancashire
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 13th-16th century
*Coords:* 53.8202, -2.4104
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whalley_Abbey
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Whalley%20Abbey/@53.8167,-2.4167,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZNYVnRxNtRN6g9KVMMfjUpCGq9eqaAgNABKedFAKLTcINaYRpOckjGQyoa7phAbtb-sWiKHFKrl3MjB2i-EHmfZDZ-Ax1t2Cdz_vdqLvrXBOMA36jGjuYkDZGYSL6VXzYjwbU6BjAwFa4wbYQ=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* https://maps.app.goo.gl/LzZSPA6wiyGusGJu6 - update google maps link 

*Description:*
Whalley Abbey is a former Cistercian abbey in Whalley, Lancashire, England. After the dissolution of the monasteries, the abbey was largely demolished and a country house was built on the site. In the 20th century the house was modified and it is now the Retreat and Conference House of the Diocese of Blackburn of the Church of England. The ruins of the abbey are recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building, and are a Scheduled Ancient Monument.

_Listing 4 of 10_

listing.name: Whalley Abbey
listing.desc: Whalley Abbey is a former Cistercian abbey in Whalley, Lancashire, England. After the dissolution of the monasteries, the abbey was largely demolished and a country house was built on the site. In the 20th century the house was modified and it is now the Retreat and Conference House of the Diocese of Blackburn of the Church of England. The ruins of the abbey are recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building, and are a Scheduled Ancient Monument.

imageAttribution.description:
Whalley Abbey, near to Billington, Lancashire, Great Britain.
		The ruins of  Whalley Abbey are a Grade I listed building and a Scheduled Ancient Monument.  The site of the abbey was first consecrated in 1306 after the Cistercian monks from Stanlow Abbey had moved to Whalley. The church was completed in 1380 but the remainder of the abbey was not finished until the 1440s. 

The abbey closed in 1537 as part of Henry VIII�s dissolution of the monasteries. Following which, the abbey was largely demolished and a country house was built on the site. In the 20th century the house was modified and it is now the Retreat and Conference House of the Diocese of Blackburn.

Today, only the foundations remain of the church. The remains of the former monastic buildings are more extensive.

<a title="http://www.whalleyabbey.co.uk/" rel="nofollow" href="http://www.whalleyabbey.co.uk/">Link</a><img style="padding-left:2px;" alt="External link" title="External link - shift click to open in new window" src="http://s0.geograph.org.uk/img/external.png" width="10" height="10"/> Official abbey web site
<a title="http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whalley_Abbey" rel="nofollow" href="http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whalley_Abbey">Link</a><img style="padding-left:2px;" alt="External link" title="External link - shift click to open in new window" src="http://s0.geograph.org.uk/img/external.png" width="10" height="10"/> Wikipedia article

🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Lowther Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Cumbria
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 19th century
*Coords:* 54.60583333, -2.74027778
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lowther_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Lowther%20Castle/@54.6167,-2.75,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZPwkua9jwGEmocaUGpLqg5J05OuP1n2hsrgNwq_RmaC1uzitjSokBnKZ3zU4hOmQKS_DpnXK3o-LtJx6ZlUDFTDNfjOOUSdh-4C8toOLASaQVwTLOctM-26eOmAVosThknVJtkb1WWbKXBB5-CeYr-y4Q=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* add source - https://lowthercastle.org/

*Description:*
Lowther Castle is a ruined country house in Lowther, Cumbria, England. The estate has belonged to the Lowther family, latterly the earls of Lonsdale, since the Middle Ages. The house was largely built between 1806 and 1814 for William Lowther, 1st Earl of Lonsdale and designed by Robert Smirke in his first major commission. It incorporates fragments of the previous house on the site, which was completed in 1685 for John Lowther, 1st Viscount Lonsdale. It is open to the public and is a grade II* listed building.

_Listing 5 of 10_
